title: Hyperosmolar Hyperglycaemic State (HHS)
oneLiner: Life-threatening hyperglycaemic emergency in type 2 diabetes; severe hyperglycaemia (>30 mmol/L) + hyperosmolality (>320 mOsm/kg) ± neurological impairment; high mortality.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Hypovolaemic shock** due to osmotic diuresis  
  - Single finding: **systolic BP <90 mmHg + HR >110** despite supine position — not just postural drop
- **Cerebral oedema / central pontine myelinolysis** from over-rapid correction of osmolality  
  - Single finding: **deteriorating GCS after starting fluids** (not improving) — stop rapid infusion, re-check Na and osmolality
- **Occlusive vascular event** (MI, stroke, mesenteric ischaemia) due to hyperviscosity  
  - Single finding: **chest/abdominal pain or focal neurology** — ECG, troponin, CT head, lactate before attributing to HHS alone
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:** polyuria, polydipsia, weight loss, lethargy, confusion
- **Key exam findings:** signs of hypovolaemia (dry mucous membranes, reduced skin turgor, tachycardia), focal neurological signs (often transient), reduced GCS (not coma in all cases), signs of precipitating infection (fever, crackles, urinary symptoms)
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA):** rapid onset, younger, T1DM; **ketones >3 mmol/L + pH <7.3 + HCO3 <15** — splitter: strong ketonuria + acidosis = DKA, not HHS
- **Hyperglycaemic crisis with sepsis:** may have overlap; look for infection source (CXR, urine culture, lactate)
- **Stroke/TIA:** focal deficits without preceding polydipsia/polyuria; glucose <30 usually; CT head + blood glucose distinguishes
- **Hypoglycaemia:** glucose <3.9 + sympathetic/neuroglycopenic symptoms; bedside glucose excludes
- **Uraemia / hyponatraemia:** slow onset, no severe hyperglycaemia; osmolar gap helps
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:** capillary glucose (stat), blood ketones (beta-hydroxybutyrate), urine dip (ketones, glucose, leucocytes), full set of observations including NEWS2, ECG
- **Bloods:** FBC, U&E (especially Na, K, creatinine), calculated osmolality (2Na + glucose + urea), measured osmolality if available, VBG (pH, HCO3, lactate, PCO2), troponin (rule out MI), blood cultures, HbA1c (baseline control)
- **Imaging:** CXR (infiltrates/oedema) first-line; CT head if GCS <13 or focal neurology persists after fluid resus
- **Special tests:** ECG mandatory — silent MI can be precipitant; consider echocardiogram if troponin elevated
- **Diagnostic criteria:**  
  - Glucose >30 mmol/L  
  - Osmolality >320 mOsm/kg (calculated or measured)  
  - pH >7.3, HCO3 >15 mmol/L  
  - Ketones <3 mmol/L  
  - Absence of significant acidosis/ketosis (mild ketones possible)
- **Never-miss test:** **ECG** — silent MI is common in elderly with HHS and drives mortality; treat underlying cause
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - Secure airway if GCS <8 or unable to protect airway
  - IV access (large bore), take bloods
  - Start IV **0.9% saline**: 1L over 30–60 minutes if hypotensive; otherwise 1L over 1 hour. Reassess after each litre.
  - **Do NOT give insulin bolus** — insulin only after fluid resus started AND K+ >3.3 mmol/L
  - Start fixed-rate IV insulin infusion (Actrapid) at **0.05–0.1 units/kg/hr** (weight-based) — do not aim for rapid glucose drop
  - Replace potassium: if K+ 3.3–5.5, add 20–40 mmol KCl per litre IV fluid (per local policy); if K+ <3.3, hold insulin and replace K+ first
  - Identify precipitant: infection (broad-spectrum abx if suspected), MI (aspirin, cardiology input), etc.
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - Continue 0.9% saline until haemodynamically stable; switch to **0.45% saline** if corrected sodium (Na + 2(glucose-5.6)/5.6) is rising or >150 mmol/L
  - Monitor glucose hourly until stable, then every 1–2 hours; target fall **3–5 mmol/L/hour** — if falling faster, reduce insulin or add 5% dextrose
  - Check electrolytes, osmolality, and VBG every 2 hours initially, then 4-hourly
  - Once glucose <15 mmol/L, switch to **5% dextrose** (e.g., 125 mL/hr) with insulin and continue fluids until osmolality <300
  - Do NOT stop insulin until metabolic stabilisation (osmolality falling, glucose stable, patient eating)
  - Initiate VTE prophylaxis (LMWH unless contraindicated) — due to hyperviscosity and immobility
  - Strict fluid balance; consider catheter if GCS <13 or fluid balance difficult
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Transition to SC insulin (e.g., basal-bolus) once eating, osmolality normalised, and no vomiting
  - Arrange diabetes specialist review for insulin/glucose-lowering regimen
  - Address precipitant and review HbA1c
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - Fluids: 0.9% saline at rate per rehydration protocol (e.g., 1L over 2-4 hours then reassess); adjust per osmolality and sodium
  - VTE prophylaxis: LMWH (e.g., enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily, adjust for renal impairment)
  - Diet/NBM: free fluids if conscious and safe swallow; NBM if GCS <13 or vomiting
  - Observations: hourly BP + HR + NEWS2, hourly neuro obs if GCS <15, hourly glucose, strict fluid balance
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line:**
  - **Insulin Actrapid IV infusion:** 0.05–0.1 units/kg/hr (weight-based) — no bolus. Adjust per glucose drop.
  - **Potassium chloride:** 20–40 mmol per litre IV fluid per serum K+ — use local protocol.
- **Common alternatives:**
  - If hypoglycaemia develops: stop insulin, give **10% dextrose** 150 mL IV, reduce insulin dose.
  - If severe hypernatraemia despite 0.9% saline: switch to **0.45% saline** (with or without 5% dextrose).
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Insulin: avoid in hypokalaemia (K+ <3.3) until corrected — risk of cardiac arrest.
  - 0.45% saline: avoid if hypotension persists or patient in shock; use cautiously in elderly with heart failure.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Insulin: reduced clearance in severe renal impairment (eGFR <30) — may require lower initial rate (0.05 units/kg/hr) and closer monitoring.
  - KCl: adjust infusion rate per renal function and urine output.
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Giving IV insulin bolus before fluid resus or with K+ <3.3** — precipitates cardiac arrest from hypokalaemia-induced arrhythmia. Second error: **aggressive glucose correction >5 mmol/L/hr** — risk of cerebral oedema and central pontine myelinolysis.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Hypovolaemic shock (due to osmotic diuresis)
  - Thrombotic events (MI, stroke, DVT/PE — hyperviscosity and dehydration)
  - Acute kidney injury (prerenal, but can become intrinsic)
  - Rhabdomyolysis (check CK if muscle pain or dark urine)
  - Cerebral oedema / central pontine myelinolysis (over-rapid osmolality correction)
  - Hypokalaemia or hyperkalaemia
  - Hypoglycaemia (from excessive insulin/dextrose mismanagement)
  - Fluid overload (especially in elderly with CKD/heart failure)
- **Chronic:**
  - Residual neurological deficits (if prolonged coma/hypoglycaemia)
  - Recurrence if precipitant (e.g., infection, poor glycemic control) not addressed
  - De novo type 2 diabetes diagnosis? — often HHS is first presentation
sections.Prognosis:
- Mortality **~10–15%**; higher if age >65, severe hyperosmolality (>350 mOsm/kg), multiple comorbidities, or complications (MI, stroke).
- Recovery depends on rapid identification and treatment of precipitant; neurological deficits may persist but often reverse with rehydration.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always check ECG** on arrival — silent MI is a common cause and driver of mortality in HHS.
- **Start fluids immediately** — even before blood results return; 1L 0.9% saline stat.
- **Delay insulin until K+ >3.3** and fluid resus initiated — prevents cardiac arrest and cerebral oedema.
- **Use corrected sodium** (Na + 2*(glucose-5.6)/5.6) to guide fluid choice — switch to 0.45% saline if rising.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do not treat HHS like DKA** — HHS has minimal ketosis; aggressive insulin without fluids leads to osmolality correction too fast. Fluids first!
- **Do not aim for rapid glucose fall** — target 3–5 mmol/L/hr. Faster drops cause cerebral oedema. Add dextrose early.
- **Do not forget the precipitant** — HHS is often triggered by infection, MI, stroke, or non-adherence. Treat the underlying cause or patient will not improve.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:** fever, chest pain, inability to eat/drink, confusion, or worsening polyuria/polydipsia.
- **Follow-up:** diabetes specialist nurse (DSN) appointment within 1–2 weeks for diabetes education and medication review; primary care follow-up for underlying comorbidity.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call ICU/medical registrar for:**
  - GCS <13 or falling
  - Persistent hypotension (SBP <90) after 2L IV fluid
  - Lactate >4 mmol/L
  - Osomolality >350 mOsm/kg despite appropriate fluids
  - Need for organ support (ventilation, inotropes, renal replacement)
  - Any sign of cerebral oedema (deteriorating neuro status after starting treatment)
- **NEWS2 ≥7** triggers rapid response team; ≥5 calls medical review.

rawMarkdown:
## Hyperosmolar Hyperglycaemic State (HHS)
**One-liner:** Life-threatening hyperglycaemic emergency in type 2 diabetes; severe hyperglycaemia (>30 mmol/L) + hyperosmolality (>320 mOsm/kg) ± neurological impairment; high mortality.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Hypovolaemic shock** due to osmotic diuresis  
  - Single finding: **systolic BP <90 mmHg + HR >110** despite supine position — not just postural drop
- **Cerebral oedema / central pontine myelinolysis** from over-rapid correction of osmolality  
  - Single finding: **deteriorating GCS after starting fluids** (not improving) — stop rapid infusion, re-check Na and osmolality
- **Occlusive vascular event** (MI, stroke, mesenteric ischaemia) due to hyperviscosity  
  - Single finding: **chest/abdominal pain or focal neurology** — ECG, troponin, CT head, lactate before attributing to HHS alone

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:** polyuria, polydipsia, weight loss, lethargy, confusion
- **Key exam findings:** signs of hypovolaemia (dry mucous membranes, reduced skin turgor, tachycardia), focal neurological signs (often transient), reduced GCS (not coma in all cases), signs of precipitating infection (fever, crackles, urinary symptoms)

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA):** rapid onset, younger, T1DM; **ketones >3 mmol/L + pH <7.3 + HCO3 <15** — splitter: strong ketonuria + acidosis = DKA, not HHS
- **Hyperglycaemic crisis with sepsis:** may have overlap; look for infection source (CXR, urine culture, lactate)
- **Stroke/TIA:** focal deficits without preceding polydipsia/polyuria; glucose <30 usually; CT head + blood glucose distinguishes
- **Hypoglycaemia:** glucose <3.9 + sympathetic/neuroglycopenic symptoms; bedside glucose excludes
- **Uraemia / hyponatraemia:** slow onset, no severe hyperglycaemia; osmolar gap helps

### Investigations
- **Bedside:** capillary glucose (stat), blood ketones (beta-hydroxybutyrate), urine dip (ketones, glucose, leucocytes), full set of observations including NEWS2, ECG
- **Bloods:** FBC, U&E (especially Na, K, creatinine), calculated osmolality (2Na + glucose + urea), measured osmolality if available, VBG (pH, HCO3, lactate, PCO2), troponin (rule out MI), blood cultures, HbA1c (baseline control)
- **Imaging:** CXR (infiltrates/oedema) first-line; CT head if GCS <13 or focal neurology persists after fluid resus
- **Special tests:** ECG mandatory — silent MI can be precipitant; consider echocardiogram if troponin elevated
- **Diagnostic criteria:**  
  - Glucose >30 mmol/L  
  - Osmolality >320 mOsm/kg (calculated or measured)  
  - pH >7.3, HCO3 >15 mmol/L  
  - Ketones <3 mmol/L  
  - Absence of significant acidosis/ketosis (mild ketones possible)
- **Never-miss test:** **ECG** — silent MI is common in elderly with HHS and drives mortality; treat underlying cause

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - Secure airway if GCS <8 or unable to protect airway
  - IV access (large bore), take bloods
  - Start IV **0.9% saline**: 1L over 30–60 minutes if hypotensive; otherwise 1L over 1 hour. Reassess after each litre.
  - **Do NOT give insulin bolus** — insulin only after fluid resus started AND K+ >3.3 mmol/L
  - Start fixed-rate IV insulin infusion (Actrapid) at **0.05–0.1 units/kg/hr** (weight-based) — do not aim for rapid glucose drop
  - Replace potassium: if K+ 3.3–5.5, add 20–40 mmol KCl per litre IV fluid (per local policy); if K+ <3.3, hold insulin and replace K+ first
  - Identify precipitant: infection (broad-spectrum abx if suspected), MI (aspirin, cardiology input), etc.
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - Continue 0.9% saline until haemodynamically stable; switch to **0.45% saline** if corrected sodium (Na + 2(glucose-5.6)/5.6) is rising or >150 mmol/L
  - Monitor glucose hourly until stable, then every 1–2 hours; target fall **3–5 mmol/L/hour** — if falling faster, reduce insulin or add 5% dextrose
  - Check electrolytes, osmolality, and VBG every 2 hours initially, then 4-hourly
  - Once glucose <15 mmol/L, switch to **5% dextrose** (e.g., 125 mL/hr) with insulin and continue fluids until osmolality <300
  - Do NOT stop insulin until metabolic stabilisation (osmolality falling, glucose stable, patient eating)
  - Initiate VTE prophylaxis (LMWH unless contraindicated) — due to hyperviscosity and immobility
  - Strict fluid balance; consider catheter if GCS <13 or fluid balance difficult
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Transition to SC insulin (e.g., basal-bolus) once eating, osmolality normalised, and no vomiting
  - Arrange diabetes specialist review for insulin/glucose-lowering regimen
  - Address precipitant and review HbA1c
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - Fluids: 0.9% saline at rate per rehydration protocol (e.g., 1L over 2-4 hours then reassess); adjust per osmolality and sodium
  - VTE prophylaxis: LMWH (e.g., enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily, adjust for renal impairment)
  - Diet/NBM: free fluids if conscious and safe swallow; NBM if GCS <13 or vomiting
  - Observations: hourly BP + HR + NEWS2, hourly neuro obs if GCS <15, hourly glucose, strict fluid balance

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line:**
  - **Insulin Actrapid IV infusion:** 0.05–0.1 units/kg/hr (weight-based) — no bolus. Adjust per glucose drop.
  - **Potassium chloride:** 20–40 mmol per litre IV fluid per serum K+ — use local protocol.
- **Common alternatives:**
  - If hypoglycaemia develops: stop insulin, give **10% dextrose** 150 mL IV, reduce insulin dose.
  - If severe hypernatraemia despite 0.9% saline: switch to **0.45% saline** (with or without 5% dextrose).
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Insulin: avoid in hypokalaemia (K+ <3.3) until corrected — risk of cardiac arrest.
  - 0.45% saline: avoid if hypotension persists or patient in shock; use cautiously in elderly with heart failure.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Insulin: reduced clearance in severe renal impairment (eGFR <30) — may require lower initial rate (0.05 units/kg/hr) and closer monitoring.
  - KCl: adjust infusion rate per renal function and urine output.
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Giving IV insulin bolus before fluid resus or with K+ <3.3** — precipitates cardiac arrest from hypokalaemia-induced arrhythmia. Second error: **aggressive glucose correction >5 mmol/L/hr** — risk of cerebral oedema and central pontine myelinolysis.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Hypovolaemic shock (due to osmotic diuresis)
  - Thrombotic events (MI, stroke, DVT/PE — hyperviscosity and dehydration)
  - Acute kidney injury (prerenal, but can become intrinsic)
  - Rhabdomyolysis (check CK if muscle pain or dark urine)
  - Cerebral oedema / central pontine myelinolysis (over-rapid osmolality correction)
  - Hypokalaemia or hyperkalaemia
  - Hypoglycaemia (from excessive insulin/dextrose mismanagement)
  - Fluid overload (especially in elderly with CKD/heart failure)
- **Chronic:**
  - Residual neurological deficits (if prolonged coma/hypoglycaemia)
  - Recurrence if precipitant (e.g., infection, poor glycemic control) not addressed
  - De novo type 2 diabetes diagnosis? — often HHS is first presentation

### Prognosis
- Mortality **~10–15%**; higher if age >65, severe hyperosmolality (>350 mOsm/kg), multiple comorbidities, or complications (MI, stroke).
- Recovery depends on rapid identification and treatment of precipitant; neurological deficits may persist but often reverse with rehydration.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always check ECG** on arrival — silent MI is a common cause and driver of mortality in HHS.
- **Start fluids immediately** — even before blood results return; 1L 0.9% saline stat.
- **Delay insulin until K+ >3.3** and fluid resus initiated — prevents cardiac arrest and cerebral oedema.
- **Use corrected sodium** (Na + 2*(glucose-5.6)/5.6) to guide fluid choice — switch to 0.45% saline if rising.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do not treat HHS like DKA** — HHS has minimal ketosis; aggressive insulin without fluids leads to osmolality correction too fast. Fluids first!
- **Do not aim for rapid glucose fall** — target 3–5 mmol/L/hr. Faster drops cause cerebral oedema. Add dextrose early.
- **Do not forget the precipitant** — HHS is often triggered by infection, MI, stroke, or non-adherence. Treat the underlying cause or patient will not improve.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:** fever, chest pain, inability to eat/drink, confusion, or worsening polyuria/polydipsia.
- **Follow-up:** diabetes specialist nurse (DSN) appointment within 1–2 weeks for diabetes education and medication review; primary care follow-up for underlying comorbidity.

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call ICU/medical registrar for:**
  - GCS <13 or falling
  - Persistent hypotension (SBP <90) after 2L IV fluid
  - Lactate >4 mmol/L
  - Osomolality >350 mOsm/kg despite appropriate fluids
  - Need for organ support (ventilation, inotropes, renal replacement)
  - Any sign of cerebral oedema (deteriorating neuro status after starting treatment)
- **NEWS2 ≥7** triggers rapid response team; ≥5 calls medical review.
